q: What are the three key facts about Canada's system of government?
opts: A republic, a direct democracy and a federation
A constitutional republic, a monarchy and a confederation
A federal state, a parliamentary democracy and a constitutional monarchy
A unitary state, a presidential democracy and a constitutional monarchy
expl: There are three key facts about Canada's system of government: our country is a federal state, a parliamentary democracy and a constitutional monarchy.

q: In which document were the responsibilities of the federal and provincial governments defined in 1867?
opts: The British North America Act, now known as the Constitution Act, 1867
The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms
expl: The responsibilities of the federal and provincial governments were defined in 1867 in the British North America Act, now known as the Constitution Act, 1867.

q: Which of the following is a responsibility of the federal government?
expl: The federal government takes responsibility for matters of national and international concern, including defence, foreign policy, interprovincial trade and communications, currency, navigation, criminal law and citizenship.

q: Which of the following is a responsibility of the provinces?
expl: The provinces are responsible for municipal government, education, health, natural resources, property and civil rights, and highways.

q: The federal government and the provinces share jurisdiction over agriculture and immigration.
expl: The guide states that the federal government and the provinces share jurisdiction over agriculture and immigration.

q: What are the three parts of Parliament?
opts: The Prime Minister, the Cabinet and the House of Commons
The Sovereign (Queen or King), the Senate and the House of Commons
The Governor General, the Supreme Court and the Senate
The House of Commons, the Senate and the provincial legislatures
expl: Parliament has three parts: the Sovereign (Queen or King), the Senate and the House of Commons.

q: What do provincial legislatures comprise?
opts: The Lieutenant Governor and the elected Assembly
The Commissioner and the elected Assembly
The Governor General and the Legislative Assembly
expl: Provincial legislatures comprise the Lieutenant Governor and the elected Assembly.

q: In the federal government, who selects the Cabinet ministers and is responsible for the operations and policy of the government?
opts: The Speaker of the House of Commons
expl: In the federal government, the Prime Minister selects the Cabinet ministers and is responsible for the operations and policy of the government.

q: How are members of the House of Commons chosen?
opts: They are appointed by the Governor General
They are appointed by the Prime Minister
They are chosen by the provincial premiers
They are elected by the people, traditionally every four years
expl: The House of Commons is the representative chamber, made up of members of Parliament elected by the people, traditionally every four years.

q: How are senators chosen?
opts: Appointed by the Governor General on the Prime Minister's advice
Elected by the people every four years
expl: Senators are appointed by the Governor General on the advice of the Prime Minister and serve until age 75.

q: Until what age do senators serve?
expl: Senators are appointed by the Governor General on the advice of the Prime Minister and serve until age 75.

q: Who grants royal assent to bills on behalf of the Sovereign?
opts: The Chief Justice of the Supreme Court
expl: No bill can become law until it has been passed by both chambers and has received royal assent, granted by the Governor General on behalf of the Sovereign.

q: A bill can become law in Canada after being passed by the House of Commons alone, without the Senate.
expl: No bill can become law in Canada until it has been passed by both chambers (the House of Commons and the Senate) and has received royal assent.

q: What happens at the First Reading stage of the legislative process?
opts: The bill is considered read for the first time and is printed
Committee members study the bill clause by clause
expl: Step 1, First Reading: the bill is considered read for the first time and is printed.

q: At which stage of the legislative process do committee members study a bill clause by clause?
expl: Step 3, Committee Stage: committee members study the bill clause by clause.

q: What is the final step before a bill becomes law?
expl: Step 7, Royal Assent: the bill receives royal assent after being passed by both Houses.

q: Who is Canada's head of state?
expl: As a constitutional monarchy, Canada's head of state is a hereditary Sovereign (Queen or King), who reigns in accordance with the Constitution: the rule of law.

q: Who is the head of government in Canada, the person who actually directs the governing of the country?
opts: The Speaker of the House of Commons
expl: There is a clear distinction between the head of state—the Sovereign—and the head of government—the Prime Minister, who actually directs the governing of the country.

q: As Head of the Commonwealth, the Sovereign links Canada to how many other nations?
expl: As Head of the Commonwealth, the Sovereign links Canada to 53 other nations that cooperate to advance social, economic and cultural progress.

q: How is the Governor General chosen?
opts: Elected by the people every five years
Appointed by the Sovereign on the advice of the Prime Minister
Chosen by a vote of the House of Commons
expl: The Sovereign is represented in Canada by the Governor General, who is appointed by the Sovereign on the advice of the Prime Minister, usually for five years.

q: Who represents the Sovereign in each of the ten provinces?
opts: The Speaker of the Legislative Assembly
expl: In each of the ten provinces, the Sovereign is represented by the Lieutenant Governor, who is appointed by the Governor General on the advice of the Prime Minister, normally for five years.

q: What are the three branches of government in Canada?
opts: The Sovereign, the Senate and the House of Commons
Parliament, the Cabinet and the Crown
expl: The interplay between the three branches of government—the Executive, Legislative and Judicial—helps to secure the rights and freedoms of Canadians.

q: Cabinet ministers must retain the "confidence of the House" and have to resign if they are defeated in a non-confidence vote.
expl: Cabinet ministers are responsible to the elected representatives, which means they must retain the confidence of the House and have to resign if they are defeated in a non-confidence vote.

q: In each province, who has a role similar to that of the Prime Minister in the federal government?
expl: In each province, the Premier has a role similar to that of the Prime Minister in the federal government, just as the Lieutenant Governor has a role similar to that of the Governor General.

q: In the three territories, what is the role of the Commissioner?
opts: To represent the federal government and play a ceremonial role
To lead the territorial government as head of government
To represent the Sovereign as head of state
expl: In the three territories, the Commissioner represents the federal government and plays a ceremonial role.

q: The three northern territories do not have the status of provinces, but their governments and assemblies carry out many of the same functions.
expl: The three northern territories, which have small populations, do not have the status of provinces, but their governments and assemblies carry out many of the same functions.

q: Depending on the province or territory, members of the legislature may be called all of the following EXCEPT:
expl: Members of provincial and territorial legislatures are called MLAs, MNAs, MPPs or MHAs (members of the House of Assembly), depending on the province or territory. Senators sit in the federal Senate.

q: How many judges sit on the Supreme Court of Canada, and who appoints them?
opts: Nine judges appointed by the Governor General
Seven judges appointed by the Prime Minister
Nine judges elected by the provinces
Twelve judges appointed by the Senate
expl: The Supreme Court of Canada has nine judges appointed by the Governor General.

q: What are the laws passed by municipal governments called?
expl: Municipal governments usually have a council that passes laws called by-laws that affect only the local community.

q: Who usually makes up a municipal council?
opts: A mayor (or a reeve) and councillors or aldermen
expl: The municipal council usually includes a mayor (or a reeve) and councillors or aldermen.

q: Which level of government is normally responsible for garbage removal, snow removal and firefighting?
expl: Municipalities are normally responsible for urban or regional planning, streets and roads, sanitation such as garbage removal, snow removal, firefighting, emergency services, recreation facilities and public transit.

q: Who has major responsibilities on First Nations reserves, including housing, schools and other services?
expl: The First Nations have band chiefs and councillors who have major responsibilities on First Nations reserves, including housing, schools and other services.

q: In Canada's constitutional monarchy, how does the Sovereign reign?
opts: By directing the day-to-day governing of the country
By commanding the House of Commons
In accordance with the Constitution: the rule of law
By appointing all members of Parliament
expl: Canada's head of state is a hereditary Sovereign (Queen or King), who reigns in accordance with the Constitution: the rule of law. The Sovereign plays an important, non-partisan role.

q: Which of the following countries is NOT listed in the guide as a constitutional monarchy?
expl: Other constitutional monarchies include Denmark, Norway, Sweden, Australia, New Zealand, The Netherlands, Spain, Thailand, Japan, Jordan and Morocco. The United States is not a monarchy.

q: The Prime Minister is Canada's head of state.
expl: The head of state is the Sovereign; the Prime Minister is the head of government, who actually directs the governing of the country.

q: Which of the following are the requirements to vote in a federal election?
opts: Be a Canadian citizen, at least 16 years old, and own property
Be a Canadian citizen, at least 18 years old on voting day, and on the voters' list
Be a permanent resident, at least 18 years old, and employed
Be a Canadian citizen, at least 21 years old, and registered with a political party
expl: You are eligible to vote in a federal election if you are a Canadian citizen, at least 18 years old on voting day, and on the voters' list.

q: From what database are the voters' lists used during federal elections produced?
expl: The voters' lists are produced from the National Register of Electors by Elections Canada, a permanent database of Canadian citizens 18 years of age or older who are qualified to vote.

q: Elections Canada is a neutral agency of Parliament.
expl: The voters' lists are produced from the National Register of Electors by a neutral agency of Parliament called Elections Canada.

q: What information is on the voter information card mailed to electors once an election has been called?
opts: When and where you vote, and a number to call for special services
The names of all candidates in your riding
Instructions on which party to vote for
expl: Elections Canada mails a voter information card to each elector in the National Register of Electors. The card lists when and where you vote and the number to call if you require an interpreter or other special services.

q: What should you do if you do not receive a voter information card?
opts: Wait until the next election to vote
Ask your employer to register you
Call your local elections office or Elections Canada
expl: If you do not receive a voter information card, call your local elections office to ensure you are on the voters' list. If you do not have the number, call Elections Canada in Ottawa.

q: If you are not on the voters' list, you cannot be added on election day.
expl: Even if you choose not to be listed in the National Register of Electors or do not receive a voter information card, you can still be added to the voters' list at any time, including on election day.

q: What does the right to a secret ballot mean?
opts: You must keep your vote secret and may never discuss it
No one can watch you vote and no one should look at how you voted
Only election officers may know how you voted
Your vote is recorded under a secret code linked to your name
expl: Canadian law secures the right to a secret ballot: no one can watch you vote and no one should look at how you voted. You may choose to discuss your vote, but no one has the right to insist you tell them.

q: By law, when must federal elections be held?
opts: On the first Tuesday of November every four years
On the third Monday of June every five years
On the third Monday in October every four years
expl: Under legislation passed by Parliament, federal elections must be held on the third Monday in October every four years following the most recent general election.

q: The Prime Minister may ask the Governor General to call an election earlier than the fixed date.
expl: The guide states that the Prime Minister may ask the Governor General to call an earlier election.

q: According to the guide, into how many electoral districts is Canada divided?
expl: The guide states that Canada is divided into 308 electoral districts, also known as ridings or constituencies.

q: What are electoral districts also known as?
expl: Canada is divided into electoral districts, also known as ridings or constituencies. An electoral district is a geographical area represented by a member of Parliament.

q: How many members of Parliament do the citizens in each electoral district elect?
opts: It depends on the population of the district
expl: The citizens in each electoral district elect one MP who sits in the House of Commons to represent them, as well as all Canadians.

q: Who may run as a candidate in a federal election?
opts: Only members of a registered political party
Canadian citizens who are 18 years old or older
expl: Canadian citizens who are 18 years old or older may run in a federal election. The people who run for office are called candidates.

q: Who becomes the MP for an electoral district after an election?
opts: The candidate chosen by the Governor General
The candidate appointed by the party with the most seats
The candidate with the most years of experience
The candidate who receives the most votes
expl: The people in each electoral district vote for the candidate and political party of their choice. The candidate who receives the most votes becomes the MP for that electoral district.

q: Ordinarily, after an election, who is invited by the Governor General to form the government?
opts: The leader of the political party with the most seats in the House of Commons
The leader of the party with the most votes nationwide
The Speaker of the House of Commons
expl: Ordinarily, after an election, the leader of the political party with the most seats in the House of Commons is invited by the Governor General to form the government, and becomes the Prime Minister.

q: What is a majority government?
opts: When the party in power holds every seat in the Senate
When the party in power wins the most votes nationally
When the party in power holds at least half of the seats in the House of Commons
When two parties agree to govern together
expl: If the party in power holds at least half of the seats in the House of Commons, this is called a majority government.

q: What is a minority government?
opts: When the party in power has fewer senators than the opposition
When the party in power holds less than half of the seats in the House of Commons
When the smallest party in Parliament forms the government
When the Governor General governs directly
expl: If the party in power holds less than half of the seats in the House of Commons, this is called a minority government.

q: How long do the Prime Minister and the party in power run the government?
opts: As long as they have the support or confidence of the majority of the MPs
For a fixed term of exactly four years, no matter what
Until the Sovereign chooses a new Prime Minister
Until the Senate votes to replace them
expl: The Prime Minister and the party in power run the government as long as they have the support or confidence of the majority of the MPs.

q: When the House of Commons votes on a major issue such as the budget, what is this considered?
expl: When the House of Commons votes on a major issue such as the budget, this is considered a matter of confidence. If the government is defeated, the Prime Minister usually asks for an election to be called.

q: What usually happens if a majority of the members of the House of Commons vote against a major government decision?
opts: The Senate takes over the government
The Prime Minister asks the Governor General to call an election
The Supreme Court appoints a new Prime Minister
The vote is simply repeated until it passes
expl: If a majority of MPs vote against a major government decision, the party in power is defeated, which usually results in the Prime Minister asking the Governor General, on behalf of the Sovereign, to call an election.

q: What are Cabinet ministers responsible for?
opts: Representing the Sovereign in the provinces
expl: Cabinet ministers are responsible for running the federal government departments. The Prime Minister and the Cabinet ministers are called the Cabinet; they prepare the budget and propose most new laws.

q: From where does the Prime Minister choose most of the ministers of the Crown?
opts: From among members of the House of Commons
From among judges of the federal courts
expl: The Prime Minister chooses the ministers of the Crown, most of them from among members of the House of Commons.

q: Which party becomes the Official Opposition (Her Majesty's Loyal Opposition)?
opts: The party that has been in Parliament the longest
The party chosen by the Governor General
The party with the most senators
The opposition party with the most members of the House of Commons
expl: The opposition party with the most members of the House of Commons is the Official Opposition or Her Majesty's Loyal Opposition.

q: What is the role of the opposition parties?
opts: To run the federal government departments
To peacefully oppose or try to improve government proposals
expl: The role of opposition parties is to peacefully oppose or try to improve government proposals.

q: According to the guide, which three major political parties are represented in the House of Commons?
opts: The Conservative Party, the New Democratic Party and the Liberal Party
The Liberal Party, the Green Party and the Reform Party
The Conservative Party, the Bloc Québécois and the Green Party
The New Democratic Party, the Labour Party and the Liberal Party
expl: The guide states there are three major political parties currently represented in the House of Commons: the Conservative Party, the New Democratic Party, and the Liberal Party.

q: What can you do if you cannot or do not wish to vote on election day?
opts: Ask a family member to vote for you
Skip the election; there is no other option
Vote at the advance polls or by special ballot
expl: If you cannot or do not wish to vote on election day, you can vote at the advance polls or by special ballot. The dates and location are on your voter information card.

q: How do you mark a federal election ballot?
opts: Write the name of your chosen party at the bottom
Mark an "X" in the circle next to the name of the candidate of your choice
Number the candidates in order of preference
Check a box beside the party leader's name
expl: Mark an "X" in the circle next to the name of the candidate of your choice. Once marked, fold the ballot and present it to the poll officials.

q: What happens immediately after the polling stations close?
opts: Election officers count the ballots and the results are made public
The ballots are sealed for one week before counting
The Governor General counts the ballots
The results are kept secret until Parliament resumes
expl: Immediately after the polling stations close, election officers count the ballots and the results are announced on radio, television and in newspapers, and on the Elections Canada website.

q: The rules for provincial, territorial and municipal elections are exactly the same as those for federal elections.
expl: Provincial, territorial and municipal elections are held by secret ballot, but the rules are not the same as those for federal elections. It is important to find out the rules so you can exercise your right to vote.

q: Your employer or union representative has the right to insist that you tell them how you voted.
expl: No one, including family members, your employer or union representative, has the right to insist that you tell them how you voted.
